subject: Science
source: Behold and See, 4th Edition - Catholic Heritage Curricula

questions.question: About how much will your brain weigh when you are fully grown?
questions.explanation: An adult brain weighs about 3 pounds, which is roughly the weight of a cantaloupe.

questions.question: What is the name of the main nerve pathway that carries messages to and from the brain?
questions.explanation: The spinal cord is a bundle of nerve fibers about as thick as a pencil that relays messages between your brain and the rest of your body.

questions.question: What are the three main parts of the brain?
options: Left brain, right brain, middle brain
questions.explanation: The three main parts are the cerebrum (thinking), cerebellum (coordination), and brain stem (automatic functions like breathing).

questions.question: Which part of the brain is responsible for intelligence, creativity, and some emotions?
questions.explanation: The cerebrum is the largest part of your brain and handles thinking, creativity, memory, and emotions.

questions.question: Which part of the brain is in charge of coordinating your muscles?
questions.explanation: The cerebellum controls coordination and balance, helping your muscles work together smoothly.

questions.question: Which part of the brain attaches to the spinal cord and controls automatic movements like breathing?
questions.explanation: The brain stem connects to the spinal cord and controls things your body does automatically, like breathing and heartbeat.

questions.question: When did your brain and nervous system begin to develop in your mother's womb?
questions.explanation: Your brain and nervous system began developing during the first three months in your mother's womb.

questions.question: What gas in the air is vital to brain cell function?
questions.explanation: Oxygen is vital for brain cells. The brain uses about 20 percent of the oxygen you breathe in!

questions.question: What are the three types of blood vessels in your body?
options: Red vessels, blue vessels, white vessels
questions.explanation: Arteries carry blood away from the heart, veins carry blood back to the heart, and tiny capillaries connect them.

questions.question: About how many red blood cells die in the adult human body every second?
questions.explanation: About 2-3 million red blood cells die every second, but the same number are made in your bone marrow each second!

questions.question: How many round trips of the body does each red blood cell make before it dies?
questions.explanation: Each red blood cell makes approximately 250,000 round trips through your body before returning to the bone marrow to die.

questions.question: How long can a single red blood cell live as it circulates through your body?
questions.explanation: Each red blood cell lives for about four months while it circulates through the body, feeding a trillion other cells.

questions.question: What is the name of the muscle that moves up and down to help the lungs breathe?
questions.explanation: The diaphragm is a dome-shaped muscle below your lungs that contracts and relaxes to help you breathe.

questions.question: What is the epiglottis?
options: A type of white blood cell
A flap that covers your windpipe when you swallow
The tube that leads to your stomach
questions.explanation: The epiglottis is a small flap that covers your windpipe (trachea) when you swallow food, so food goes to your stomach instead of your lungs.

questions.question: What are the tiny air sacs in your lungs called?
questions.explanation: Alveoli are tiny air sacs in your lungs where oxygen enters your blood and carbon dioxide leaves. You have hundreds of millions of them!

questions.question: What are the tubes called that branch off into each lung?
questions.explanation: The bronchi are two tubes that branch off from the trachea (windpipe) and lead into each lung.

questions.question: Approximately how many times do children breathe per minute when resting?
questions.explanation: Children breathe about 20-30 times per minute when resting, which is faster than adults.

questions.question: What gas does your body exhale as a waste product?
questions.explanation: Your body breathes out carbon dioxide, which is a waste product made when your cells use oxygen for energy.

questions.question: Which lung is slightly larger than the other?
options: They are exactly the same size
questions.explanation: The right lung is slightly larger than the left lung because the left side of your chest needs to make room for your heart.

questions.question: What is the name of the voice box in your throat?
questions.explanation: The larynx (voice box) contains your vocal cords, which vibrate when air passes over them to make sounds.

questions.question: How does a baby receive oxygen in the womb?
options: Through the umbilical cord from the mother
questions.explanation: A baby receives oxygen through the umbilical cord, which connects the baby to the mother's blood supply.

questions.question: How many bones are in the adult human body?
questions.explanation: Adults have 206 bones. Babies are born with about 270 bones, but some fuse together as you grow.

questions.question: What is the largest and strongest bone in your body?
questions.explanation: The femur (thigh bone) is the longest, largest, and strongest bone in your body.

questions.question: What hidden substance inside your bones makes new blood cells?
questions.explanation: Bone marrow is the soft, jelly-like substance inside your bones that produces new blood cells.

questions.question: What is the name for the place where two bones meet and can bend or move?
questions.explanation: A joint is where two or more bones meet. Joints allow your body to bend and move in different ways.

questions.question: What strong, flexible material covers the ends of bones at a joint?
questions.explanation: Cartilage is a smooth, flexible material that cushions the ends of bones so they don't grind against each other.

questions.question: What are the strong bands of tissue called that help hold joints in place?
questions.explanation: Ligaments are tough, elastic bands of tissue that connect bones to other bones and help hold joints together.

questions.question: What is the helmet-shaped bone that protects your brain?
questions.explanation: The cranium (skull) is a helmet-shaped bone that surrounds and protects your brain.

questions.question: How many pairs of ribs protect your heart and lungs?
questions.explanation: You have 12 pairs of ribs (24 total) that form a cage to protect your heart and lungs.

questions.question: Approximately how many skeletal muscles do you have in your body?
questions.explanation: You have approximately 640 skeletal muscles in your body that help you move.

questions.question: What are the three different kinds of muscle tissue?
questions.explanation: The three types are skeletal (moves your bones), smooth (in organs like your stomach), and cardiac (your heart muscle).

questions.question: What is a tendon?
options: A tough cord that connects muscle to bone
questions.explanation: A tendon is a tough, rope-like cord that attaches your muscles to your bones so muscles can move your skeleton.

questions.question: What kind of muscle is the heart?
questions.explanation: The heart is made of cardiac muscle, a special type of muscle found only in the heart that never gets tired.

questions.question: What is the difference between voluntary and involuntary muscles?
options: You control voluntary muscles, but involuntary muscles work on their own
questions.explanation: Voluntary muscles (like your arm muscles) are ones you choose to move. Involuntary muscles (like your heart and stomach) work automatically without you thinking about it.

questions.question: How are messages sent to your muscles so they can do their job?
questions.explanation: Your brain sends electrical messages through your nerves to tell your muscles when and how to move.

questions.question: How do we strengthen our muscles?
questions.explanation: Muscles get stronger when you exercise and use them regularly. The more you work a muscle, the stronger it becomes.

questions.question: What does 'digestion' mean?
options: The process of breaking down food so your body can use it
The movement of blood through your body
questions.explanation: Digestion is the process of breaking down the food you eat into nutrients your body can absorb and use for energy.

questions.question: What is the name of the tube that leads from your throat to your stomach?
questions.explanation: The esophagus is a muscular tube that pushes food from your throat down to your stomach using wave-like contractions.

questions.question: What are the two purposes of saliva in digestion?
options: Moistening food and beginning to break it down
Warming food and changing its color
questions.explanation: Saliva moistens food to make it easier to swallow and contains enzymes that begin breaking down starches.

questions.question: Which is longer, the small intestine or the large intestine?
questions.explanation: The small intestine is much longer (about 20 feet!) even though it's called 'small' because it's narrower than the large intestine.

questions.question: What is the body's largest internal organ?
questions.explanation: The liver is the body's largest internal organ, weighing about 3 pounds. It performs over 500 functions!

questions.question: Which organ produces insulin, a hormone that regulates sugar in the blood?
questions.explanation: The pancreas produces insulin, which helps control how much sugar is in your blood.

questions.question: What is bile and what does it do?
options: A bone that protects the stomach
A fluid that helps break down fats
questions.explanation: Bile is a fluid made by the liver and stored in the gallbladder that helps break down fats in the food you eat.

questions.question: Where are the kidneys located in your body?
options: In your lower back, one on each side of the spine
questions.explanation: Your two kidneys are located in your lower back, one on each side of your spine.

questions.question: What two things do the kidneys do for the body?
options: Filter waste from the blood and balance water levels
Break down fat and build muscle
questions.explanation: The kidneys filter waste products out of your blood and help balance the amount of water in your body.

questions.question: About how much of your body mass is water?
questions.explanation: About 60% of your body weight is water. More than half of you is water!

questions.question: What is the name of the muscular bag that stretches to hold liquid waste?
questions.explanation: The bladder is a muscular bag that stretches to store urine until you are ready to go to the bathroom.

questions.question: How many ureters do you have, and what do they do?
options: One, it connects the kidneys to the brain
Two, they carry urine from the kidneys to the bladder
Two, they carry blood to the heart
questions.explanation: You have two ureters, one from each kidney, that carry urine from the kidneys to the bladder.

questions.question: Humans have the same number of neck bones as which tall animal?
questions.explanation: Humans and giraffes both have 7 neck bones (cervical vertebrae). The giraffe's are just much longer!

questions.question: How many muscles does it take to frown?
questions.explanation: It takes 34 muscles to frown but only 13 to smile. Keep smiling!

didYouKnow.fact: Your brain contains more water than blood. About 85 percent of your brain is water!
Your brain uses about 20 percent of all the oxygen you breathe in!
Your brain runs on the same amount of electrical power as a 20-watt lightbulb, but it's far more complex than any computer!
Your spinal cord is a bundle of nerve fibers that is about as thick as a pencil!
About 2-3 million red blood cells die every second, and the same number are made in your bone marrow each second!
Within a single tiny droplet of blood, there are about 5 million red blood cells!
Each red blood cell makes about 250,000 trips around your body before it wears out!
Your right lung is slightly larger than your left because your left side needs room for your heart!
The surface area of your lungs, including all the air channels, is roughly the same size as half a tennis court!
You lose about two cups of water every day just by breathing! That's the water vapor you see when you breathe on a cold window.
People who live high up in the mountains have larger lungs so they can take in more of the thinner air!
Humans and giraffes have the same number of neck bones (7). The giraffe's are just much, much longer!
The smallest bones in your body are found in your ear!
When you are fully grown, your bones will be stronger than granite rock!
If you walk one mile a day starting at age 10, you'll travel about 22,000 miles in your lifetime. That's about 50 million steps!
The only bone in your body not connected to another bone is the hyoid, a U-shaped bone that holds your tongue!
Over the next 24 hours, the muscles in both of your eyes will move more than 100,000 times!
Taking just a single step uses about 200 muscles in your body!
It takes 34 muscles to frown but only 13 to smile. Keep smiling!
Without your muscles, your heart would not beat, you could not eat or breathe, and you could not move!
You eat about 1,885 pounds of food per year. That's about 60,000 pounds of food in a lifetime!
Muscles squeeze food down your esophagus in waves. Food would reach your stomach even if you were standing on your head!
Your liver performs over 500 functions, most of which involve controlling what should and shouldn't be in your blood!
An adult's stomach can hold approximately 1.5 quarts of food and liquid!
If your kidneys stopped working, waste substances would build up in your blood and poison your body within a few days!
More than half of your body weight is water!
